Hi, just to confirm:
W = width = columns
H = height = rows
C = channels
N = number of filters.

I think the most important thing here is clarity and consistency with the standard DyNet API, so H should come before W, and it makes sense that C comes after HW. N could come either first or last, as both make sense.

So in summary, I think NHWC is the most intuitive option here. Even if the performance is a little bit worse on CuDNN, I think we should make sure that we go with consistency and simplicity of the API, which is one of the core design principles behind DyNet.

I've mentioned several ideas in this thread, and I think all those that do not affect the logic of the OTL features for a given instance can be offloaded to a different process, e.g. the creation of the positioning deltas across variation masters. This can be indeed done by providing several separate FEA files which a tool "blends", like Google's fontmake. We don't *have to* change the FEA syntax for that. 

But to get reasonable support for FeatureVariations, I don't see how this can be easily without extending FEA. 

Imagine an Arabic font which in the "wdth" axis only adjusts the width of the kashida stretching glyphs and of stretchable Arabic letters that have the horizontal descenders. In addition to that, the designer might want to gradually switch on stacking ligatures, so glyph sequences that join horizontally if there is ample horizontal space would start joining vertically if there is less space. Such a strategy could be used for other cursive scripts, and even Latin fonts. 

Similarly, we might want to enable other contextual substitutions when a certain width or weight threshold is exceeded. 

For that, proper support for the FeatureVariations conditions would be necessary, especially in GSUB. 

Implementing conditional switching of GPOS adjustments in kern/mark and combining it with the blending of GPOS may indeed be tricky and perhaps out of scope, though the syntax I proposed would still be compatible and allow it in theory. 

Whether it'll be MakeOTF or feaLib or FontForge that implements the syntax changes first, or ever, is another story.

> Since there seems to be some demand for it, I think we could add a separate tool (i.e. "pyftconvert") that only does the conversion between different "flavors", so one doesn't need to first decompile/dump the font to TTX, or use pyftsubset to do that (/cc @behdad).

Ok PTAL. The binary will create a backup in case caching fails, and attempt to restore the cache on failure. There are also some changes to the utils file, since I found a discrepancy between what the library expected for page_token behavior, and what actually happens in production.

The library thought that the last page would contain an empty page token, along with the final array of passwd entries. What actually happens is the last page of actual passwd entries still has a page token, and the *next* page will contain no passwd entries, but a page token of 0.

Just took a quick stab at it and I don't think `validators.py` will work. If one passes a `<troposphere.ec2.NatGateway>` object, then it would be straight forward. But in your example, and in how most people build troposphere scripts, you passed a `<troposphere.Ref>` object. This only returns the string name of the object being referenced (as I understand it). The validator would have to have a higher perspective on the script being written to be able to trace the string to the object. 

I am not sure troposphere is the right place to solve this problem, at least with its current features. If you have another idea for solving this problem, that would be helpful.

 * Change 'GatewayId' in `ec2.py`:

```
class Route(AWSObject):

    props = {
        'DestinationCidrBlock': (basestring, True),

        'InstanceId': (basestring, False),
        'NatGatewayId': (basestring, False),
```

```
def gatewayobjstring(x):
    """

    """
    if type(x) is str:
        return x
    elif type(x) is troposphere.ec2.NatGateway:
        raise ValueError("%r is not valid. NatGW is not IGW or VGW" % x)
    elif type(x) is troposphere.Ref:
        if we_could_only_tell_what_the_original_object_was:
    else:
        return x
```

Yup, you have a python encoding issue.  If I'm interpreting your screen 
shot correctly, you have a STRING object that's full of non-ASCII 
characters.  So that string is encoded.  You have to add a 
.decode(encoding) to it to convert it to a unicode object, and to do 
that, you have to know what encoding it uses.  I suspect your 
development environment is automatically decoding the string to present 
it to you.  type(title) should show you have a "str". 
type(title.decode(encoding)) should show "unicode".

Your "title" variable that's hard-coded is a unicode object, not a 
string object.  You could .encode(encoding) it to convert it to a 
string, but in this case, you don't want to.  type(title) should show 
you have a "unicode".

So in the case of your latest screen shot, I think you have to decode() 
the string and use that.

Predefined should be designed such that the predefined PanObjects are separate from the other PanObjects.  Otherwise it would be too confusing to know which are read-only (predefined) and which are writable.  I think this would be best accomplished by a singleton-style submodule attached to the PanDevice class, similar to the UserID and Upgrader objects.  This would allow developers to pull predefined objects from the PanDevice, then customize and add them to the PanDevice configuration tree (for example, to create a custom applications based on a predefined application).  This plan would also keep predefined PanObjects out of the object tree, since they are not handled the same way as normal PanObjects, having them in the same object tree would break recursive operations on the tree.

Since the predefined XML is extremely large, there should be a focus on pre-filtering as much as possible to reduce the size of the XML responses to API calls.

Does this design sound like it would fit your use case?

I think we're getting closer for sure. The basic idea is that I'd rather have configuration be specified at runtime rather than "installed." That might be because I have two separate virtual environments, it might be because I want to include my configuration in source control, etc. The more pieces of using this package that are hard to specify in source control, the harder it is to use in a build process/CI.

When we use the `import sascfg` syntax, it's sensitive to sys.path and to where you're running python. If instead we allowed for the option of specifying the location of the file, you'd know precisely what configuration you were using.

The goal of making the autoconfig functionality work is the same regardless though, so we're on the same page there. Whether the configuration is hard-coded into sascfg.py or read in from a config file, `init_config()` seems like a good idea to me.

Yes, there is.  If you pass the `start_tokens` and `end_tokens` flags to the `WordTokenizer`, we'll automatically add whatever (string) tokens you want:
https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/55b0411bea8ae241332aa6c96cc9b4cf189afe20/allennlp/data/tokenizers/word_tokenizer.py#L34-L37

You can see how this is done in our BiDAF configuration here:
https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/55b0411bea8ae241332aa6c96cc9b4cf189afe20/training_config/bidaf.json#L13-L14
though note that this uses integers instead of strings, because we're using byte encoding for characters.

Depending on exactly what you're trying to do, though, this may or may not be what you want to do.  For language modeling or seq2seq models, for instance, you might want to handle things differently, because these tokens actually have special meaning during decoding.

I'm looking into whether this PR may actually be unnecessary now that we have upgraded to etcd2...

From etcd docs:

> etcd can now run as a transparent proxy. Running etcd as a proxy allows for easily discovery of etcd within your infrastructure, since it can run on each machine as a local service. In this mode, etcd acts as a reverse proxy and forwards client requests to an active etcd cluster. The etcd proxy does not participant in the consensus replication of the etcd cluster, thus it neither increases the resilience nor decreases the write performance of the etcd cluster.

And:

> If you bootstrap a etcd cluster using discovery service with more than the expected number of etcd members, the extra etcd processes will fall back to being readwrite proxies by default. They will forward the requests to the cluster as described above. For example, if you create a discovery url with size=5, and start ten etcd processes using that same discovery url, the result will be a cluster with five etcd members and five proxies. Note that this behaviour can be disabled with the proxy-fallback flag.

~~~

It does (if they are specified in `setup.py`), but `conda-build` overwrites those if they are also given in `meta.yaml`. In either case, with `setuptools` or `conda-build`, you can, of course, always create your own scripts instead of using the generated ones.

@ukoethe a few pointers:

1. setup.py install does not put the cmd folder in the right place unless you also tell it not to put stuff into an egg.  I have been using:

    python setup.py install --old-and-unmanageable

2.  You need to run the tests in the conda root folder, and be sure that you have installed pytest-cov.  Alternatively, you can remove or rename setup.cfg to not run coverage.

3.  To run test_activate.py you need to specify shells.  here's an example for windows (assumes that you have installed git bash with ```conda install git```):

    py.test tests\test_activate.py --shell=cmd.exe --shell=git_bash

More details in this comment: https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/1727#issuecomment-179923341

Oh, I think I see what happened. I didn't notice that you started off by conda installing R. The way both bash and zsh work is that when you type a command, like `R`, it searches the paths in `PATH` one by one until it finds it, and then it caches the location (this is called "hashing" in shell terminology), so that when you type it again, it doesn't have to search the `PATH` again, it just runs it. 

What probably happened was you started R before you conda installed it, which loaded and hashed the one in `/usr/bin`. Then you conda installed it, but the shell still had `R` hashed to `/usr/bin/R`. 

When you run `source activate`, it automatically runs `hash -r` in bash and `rehash` in zsh to clear the hashed commands, so it will find things in the new path on the PATH, but there is no way to do this when a `conda install` is run (the command has to be run inside the shell itself, meaning either you have to type it yourself or source a file that does it). 

This is a relatively rare gotcha, since it will only happen if you activate an environment (or just use the root environment), run a command from somewhere else, then conda install it and try to run it again without running `source activate` or `source deactivate`. But it's worth knowing about. `type command_name` will always tell you exactly what is being run (this is better than `which command_name`, which ignores hashed commands and searches the `PATH` directly), and `hash -r ` (in bash) or `rehash` (in zsh) will reset the hash so that you get the right thing. Or you can just run `source activate`, as it does this as well.

> MD5s are too fragile to rely on

I merely suggested to *record* URLs and MD5s of the build dependencies in `index.json`, but didn't say anything about what they might be used for :smile:. I agree that they are probably too fragile for version resolution. But they can be handy for analytic tools (sanitizers, debuggers) that people may implement in the future to track down compatibility issues in conda environments (for example, if someone uploads a new package variant but forgets to bump the build number, of if two channels provide packages with the same name (including build string) but different contents). Recording URLs and MD5s for 'just in case' purposes doesn't look wrong to me, since it costs next to nothing to do so.

@ilanschnell what has happened here seems to be a `cas-installer` issue perhaps, but I'd say linked to what `conda` allows you to do (or perhaps `cas-installer` is doing something nefarious).  Basically the Anaconda *base* results in 2.7.10-0 being installed, but then due to `cas-installer` we immediately update it via the bundled tarball to 2.7.10-1 being installed.  But conda doesn't see that this replaces/updates 2.7.10-0, so it thinks *both* are installed (which should never happen).  Then at some later point `conda install` or `conda update` builds a package plan that specifies the newest version of python (2.7.10-1), sees that an old version is installed (2.7.10-0), assumes only one version is installed, adds to the installation plan the action to remove the files listed against the old version (2.7.10-0), but at that point does notice that 2.7.10-1 *is* installed already, so it doesn't add to the plan that 2.7.10-1 should be installed (since it is there before the install starts, but doesn't realize that the associated files are about to be blown away: at least the ones that overlap with the old version, which is almost all of them).

The cookiecutter thing looks unrelated (the issue there is that it's not been built for Python 2.7).

```
        import collections
        res = collections.Counter()
        for r in read_iterator:

                for read_loc, genome_loc in r.get_aligned_pairs():
                    if read_loc is None and last_read_pos:
                        start = genome_loc
                    elif read_loc and last_read_pos is None:
                        stop = genome_loc  # we are right exclusive ,so this is correct
                        res[(start, stop)] += 1
                        del start
                        del stop
                    last_read_pos = read_loc
```

@lostllama yes, I'm well aware of this bug and intending to fix it for the next `az` release. (This behavior was inherited from `az acs kubernetes install-cli` BTW.)

Dropping a binary into the `%Path%` without requiring an Admin shell on Windows is tricky. Using [`chocolatey`](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/tools/install-kubectl/#install-with-chocolatey-on-windows) is actually the recommended way, but we can't assume that's set up on every box. My ideas are:
1. Try to determine where `az` lives and put `kubectl` in the same directory
2. Force the user to specify a full path for where `kubectl.exe` should be saved
3. Keep the default of `C:\Program Files\kubectl.exe` and then use that full path in the `az aks browse` command
4. Keep the default of `C:\Program Files\kubectl.exe` but then admonish the user to move the binary somewhere in the `%Path`
5. Modify the actual `%Path%` environment variable to ensure the target directory is searched (probably impossible since I don't think `win32api` and other native packages are installed)

We can discuss the new pull request on the other thread; here I just want to make sure we answer all your questions!

Communicating with the storage team:
A great way to contact us is to open an issue, or comment on an existing issue. In this case we already had an [issue](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/252) open to track the file service feature which would’ve been a great place to start a discussion. Issues are also a great way to ask for a feature, whether or not you intend on contributing code. 

Restructuring changes:
This reorganization in part to facilitate the storage code moving to a new repository but also to reorganize a code base that has grown a lot since its inception. With the proliferation of storage services (ex file) we thought this would also be a good time to separate the storage services into their own subpackages. We do realize this is a break, but we hope a more organized package system will make our API more usable and extensible in the future. As Hugues mentioned, dev is currently not stable and part of that instability is in the error classes. There may be breaks, but we’re not sure yet exactly where things will land.

Further projects:
I personally don’t know much about azurectl but if you’d like to chat with storage folks at any point about it, feel free to email jahogg at microsoft dotcom and we can set up a call. Per above, if you have specific contributions in mind opening an issue is the best way to go. 

Best,
Emily

Bullet loadURDF command ignores the inertia tensor by default, since it is often a bogus value. Instead, it recomputes the inertia based on mass and volume of the collision shape. You can force using the inertia from the URDF file using the flag URDF_USE_INERTIA_FROM_FILE. Even if you use the C-API, it helps reading the pybullet.org documentation, since pybullet uses the same C-API.

```
int flags = URDF_USE_INERTIA_FROM_FILE;
b3LoadUrdfCommandSetFlags(command,flags);
```

Regexps are more powerful, but with their power they move part of data validation on router side. Say, `/user/:id` vs `/user/{id:\d+}` - placeholder knowns nothing about what ids are valid and delegates their check to handler side. Regular expression tries to ensure that they are numeric ones. Thanks for this! However, I am extremely unwilling to merge this: sorry!

Ultimately, the purpose of certifi is not to be a cross-platform module to access the system certificate store. Such a thing is a *remarkably* tricky thing to build sensibly. I've discussed this informally a number of times in the Python community, but in general it is very difficult to get safe, system-native behaviour by passing OpenSSL a copy of the system trust roots. OpenSSL validates certificates differently than the system does, and it is entirely possible to get situations where OpenSSL validates a chain that the system would reject. This behaviour is subtle and surprising, and it's vastly better to resolve it by simply handing the remote cert chain off to the system trust library to verify.

On top of that, this makes certifi behave differently on different systems. That's surprising, and not really what we're going for. It would be entirely reasonable to have a meta package that does what you've proposed here (modulo the dangers mentioned in the previous paragraph) and then falls back to certifi on systems where it cannot use the extensions.

Ultimately, I think the better approach is simply to put our efforts behind [PEP543](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0543/), which would hopefully obviate the need for certifi altogether by enabling Python tools to behave like curl, and use the most appropriate TLS library for the platform they run on.

Sorry again, but thanks for your contribution!

Cleanest way to solve this for both importing students and importing assignments was to create a generic superclass. 

That superclass requires you to specify the sqlalchemy class from the `api` module that it updates as one of its attributes. 
For `DbStudentImportApp`, that is `Student`.  
For `DbAssignmentImportApp`, that is `Assignment`. 

This also involves specifying the method used for the update on the `Gradebook` `update_or_create_student` for `Student`, `update_or_create_assignment` for `Assignment`) and the key used in that method to identify the particular instance in the table (`'id'` for `Student`, `'name'` for `Assignment`).

- you should use the true label, not the one predicted by the, then you might have caught these mistakes earlier

Something like this for carray_block_vstack:

```Python
def carray_block_vstack(tup, blen=None, **kwargs):
    assert isinstance(tup, (tuple, list)), 'first argument must be tuple or list'
    assert len(tup) > 1, 'provide two or more arrays to stack'
    a = tup[0]
    for x in tup[1:]:
        assert x.shape[1:] == a.shape[1:], 'arrays must have equal size for trailing dimensions'
        assert len(x.shape) == len(a.shape), 'arrays must have same dimensionality'

    # set block size to use
    if blen is None:
        blen = min([x.chunklen for x in tup])

    # output defaults
    kwargs.setdefault('dtype', a.dtype)
    kwargs.setdefault('cparams', getattr(a, 'cparams', None))
    kwargs.setdefault('expectedlen', sum(x.shape[0] for x in tup))

    # setup output
    out = None

    # build output
    for a in tup:
        for i in range(0, a.shape[0], blen):
            block = a[i:i+blen]
            if out is None:
                out = bcolz.carray(block, **kwargs)
            else:
                out.append(block)

    return out
```

My pleasure for the package. Will keep you informed.

> why didn't you guys use regular git submodules?

For my part, because basically they come with many [pitfalls](https://www.google.ca/?gws_rd=ssl#q=why+git+submodules+are+bad) and are not meant
for our use case (sub modules would change too often for the workflow
being practical), do not work seamlessly with standard git command and are also
more limited in terms of supported vcs types.

In its actual incarnation, it seem from my POV that the `peru` is doing part
of the job done by a package manager such as `nix`. The package manager
will, for every dependency: fetch the sources, patch them, build them,  and install them. In
the case of `nix`, it even allows you to do so in sandboxes (on a per project basis).

`peru` is actually performing the fetching part and a kind of patching / reorganizing part.
A bit more tailored at a development setup (by default fetching latest for all) but without the 
hackability offered by other tools such as `repo`, `mr`, etc.

@dmasad, I just figured that the deterministic run problem can be resolved with `PYTHONHASHSEED=0` (>=3.2.3).
I tested
```
PYTHONHASHSEED=0 python3.5 -c 'print({"GO:0000054": "ribosomal subunit export from nucleus","GO:0000278": "mitotic cell cycle","GO:0000746": "conjugation","GO:0000902": "cell morphogenesis","GO:0000910": "cytokinesis"})'
{'GO:0000746': 'conjugation', 'GO:0000910': 'cytokinesis', 'GO:0000278': 'mitotic cell cycle', 'GO:0000902': 'cell morphogenesis', 'GO:0000054': 'ribosomal subunit export from nucleus'}
```
I repeated more than 30 times, with all the result having the same order. If this were to happen by chance, the probability would be (0.25**30 = 8.67e-19).

Since there is no way to set `PYTHONHASHSEED` from a library, the only way to do this is via `os.environ['PYTHONHASHSEED'] = 0`.

Each worker acts a separate session, collecting all tests and sending their ids to the master node, which in turn selects which tests run in each worker. Please take a look at [OVERVIEW](
https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-xdist/blob/master/OVERVIEW.md) for more details.

Looking at the description nothing obvious strikes me that could explain your problem. The `conftest.py` file (which is located at the root of your installation? That's unusual) is imported by each worker, so the "path" setting should work.

I suggest to print the contents of `sys.path` before attempting to import `sdss_access` in `conftest.py` to try to pinpoint the problem:

```python
import pytest
from marvin import config, marvindb
import sys
print('sys.path:')
for x in sys.path: print(x)
from sdss_access.path import Path
```

Hi Laszlo,
That is odd, prime256v1 (a.k.a. secp256r1 and NIST P-256, depending on standards org), is the correct curve to use. That public key I used actually came from openssl. Here's the procedure I used to generated it:

Generate a key pair and generate the SubjectPublicKeyInfo for elliptic curve public keys per RFC5480:
```bash
$ openssl ecparam -name prime256v1 -genkey -out private-key.pem
$ openssl ec -in private-key.pem -pubout -outform DER -out public-key.der
```

The short answer is to just use the last 64-bytes of the public-key.der output file from the last command.
```bash
$ hexdump public-key.der
0000000 30 59 30 13 06 07 2a 86 48 ce 3d 02 01 06 08 2a
0000010 86 48 ce 3d 03 01 07 03 42 00 04 b2 be 34 5a d7
0000020 89 93 83 a9 aa b4 fb 96 8b 1c 78 35 cb 2c d4 2c
0000030 7e 97 c2 6f 85 df 8e 20 1f 3b e8 a8 29 83 f0 a1
0000040 1d 6f f3 1d 66 ce 99 32 46 6f 0f 2c ca 21 ef 96
0000050 be c9 ce 23 5b 3d 87 b0 f8 fa 9e              
000005b
```
Public key is:
```
b2 be 34 5a d7 89 93 83 a9 aa b4 fb 96 8b 1c 78 35 cb 2c d4 2c 7e 97 c2 6f 85 df 8e 20 1f 3b e8 a8 29 83 f0 a1 1d 6f f3 1d 66 ce 99 32 46 6f 0f 2c ca 21 ef 96 be c9 ce 23 5b 3d 87 b0 f8 fa 9e
```

For the long answer, we have to look at the raw X and Y integers that compose an elliptic curve public key. We can use openssl to dump the ASN.1 DER encoded SubjectPublicKeyInfo to see the X and Y values.
```bash
$ openssl asn1parse -dump -inform DER -in public-key.der 
    0:d=0  hl=2 l=  89 cons: SEQUENCE          
    2:d=1  hl=2 l=  19 cons: SEQUENCE          
    4:d=2  hl=2 l=   7 prim: OBJECT            :id-ecPublicKey
   13:d=2  hl=2 l=   8 prim: OBJECT            :prime256v1
   23:d=1  hl=2 l=  66 prim: BIT STRING        
      0000 - 00 04 b2 be 34 5a d7 89-93 83 a9 aa b4 fb 96 8b   ....4Z..........
      0010 - 1c 78 35 cb 2c d4 2c 7e-97 c2 6f 85 df 8e 20 1f   .x5.,.,~..o... .
      0020 - 3b e8 a8 29 83 f0 a1 1d-6f f3 1d 66 ce 99 32 46   ;..)....o..f..2F
      0030 - 6f 0f 2c ca 21 ef 96 be-c9 ce 23 5b 3d 87 b0 f8   o.,.!.....#[=...
      0040 - fa 9e  
```
The X and Y integers are in an unsigned big-endian fixed-width format. Integers will be the same as the key size, for the prime256v1 (NIST P-256) curve that will be 32 bytes. I've broken down the BIT STRING above into the following components:
Bit string unused bits: ```00```
Key compression byte: ```04```
X integer: ```b2 be 34 5a d7 89 93 83 a9 aa b4 fb 96 8b 1c 78 35 cb 2c d4 2c 7e 97 c2 6f 85 df 8e 20 1f 3b e8```
Y integer: ```a8 29 83 f0 a1 1d 6f f3 1d 66 ce 99 32 46 6f 0f 2c ca 21 ef 96 be c9 ce 23 5b 3d 87 b0 f8 fa 9e```

The key compression byte, 0x04, indicates an uncompressed public key. We don’t support or use the compressed format. More information can be found in [RFC5480 section 2.2](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5480#section-2.2), which pulls from [SECG SEC1](http://www.secg.org/sec1-v2.pdf) section 2.3.3.

The first raw format the ATECCx08A uses, is the X and Y integers concatenated together, exactly as found in the bit string (after the 0x04 byte), for a total of 64 bytes.
```
b2be345ad7899383a9aab4fb968b1c7835cb2cd42c7e97c26f85df8e201f3be8a82983f0a11d6ff31d66ce9932460f2cca21ef96bec9ce235b3d87b0f8fa9e
```
This is the format returned by the GenKey command when creating a new key pair or requesting the public key of an existing private key. It is also what is supplied to the Verify and ECDH commands.

The other format is only used when storing a public key in a slot. In this case, the X and Y integers are padded out to a 36 byte size for a total of 72 bytes.
```
00000000b2be345ad7899383a9aab4fb968b1c7835cb2cd42c7e97c26f85df8e201f3be800000000a82983f0a11d6ff31d66ce9932460f2cca21ef96bec9ce235b3d87b0f8fa9e
```

More detail on this:

If a Subscriber requests example.com, we should attempt to include www.example.com as well. If a Subscriber requests www.example.com, we should attempt to include example.com as well.

'Attempt to include' == First, DNS lookup the requested name and store the result. Second, DNS lookup the other name and compare the result. If they are the same, proceed to attempt authorization for both names. Otherwise, attempt authorization only for the requested name.

I would suggest that this should be opt-out, with an optional flag --no-www.

```
#------------------------------------------------------------------------------#
```

Suppose following node-topic bounds happens with each user never had any p2p connection with each other (I used new users to ensure this property):

* Node 1:
    * User A
* Node 2:
    * User B
* Node 3:
    * User C
    
When Node 1 dies, failover occurs, then suppose the result would be following:

* Node 2:
    * User B
    * User A
* Node 3:
    * User C

When User C try to initiate p2p connection with User A, it would get `Permission Denied` error. But when User B try to initiate p2p connection with User A, it will be successfully created. But when we add more users to both Node 2 & Node 3, sometimes this event would be occurred, sometimes it doesn't. But if the user bound to Node 3, then it more likely to get the error when it tried to initiate connection with User A. This bug also sometimes occurs without failover event.

The second bug was occurred when I delete p2p subscription which topic is bound to another node then recreate it. So for example User B successfully created p2p connection with User C, then User B delete the subscription, then recreate it. There possible outcome would be 3 following cases: 
* when it is successfully recreated, user own permissions would only have 'A' permission
* when it is successfully recreated, user own permissions would have 'N' permission, so does with user in another side
* when it is unsuccessfully recreated, it throws "Permission Denied" error to the user

Let me know your opinion.

See my reasoning in #350. I don't think this is a good idea. The problem with these things is that they only *sometimes* work, which leads to much harder-to-diagnose problems than if they *always* fail. Also, if you want to do some operations on things that are entirely `timedelta`-like, why not just use a `timedelta` in the first place?

It's not a problem for users to subclass `relativedelta` to have any sort of ordering semantics they want, and that *is* something we'll support going forward.

Additionally, they can easily define an "order" function like this:

```python
def rd_lt(rd1, rd2):
    dt = datetime(1970, 1, 1)
    return (dt + rd1) < (dt + rd2)
```

In any case, I think `relativedelta` is a bit of a weird class in itself, and is probably widely misused. I'm in the middle of refactoring it into `calendardelta` and `partialdate`. I'm thinking that `calendardelta` might have a `to_timedelta()` method, with some mechanism for specifying how ambiguous deltas are handled, which should be sufficient for ordering purposes.

Thanks @danpoland for #82 but it runs into issues when writing data. Also there is no way to handle one-to-many relationships.

How I usually design my schemas is to set ``include_resource_linkage`` to ``True`` in case of ``to-one`` relationships and ``False`` in case of ``to-many`` relationships. In ``to-many`` case I would like to just put the links for the user to fetch resources when needed.

Right now I am subclassing the ``Relationship`` class with following ``Relationship`` class which handles almost all the use cases I can think of.

```python
class Relationship(flask.Relationship):
    """Relationship object

    It ensures that the Relationship does not load Model lazy relationships
    unless asked for, thus making the responses a bit faster.

    ``attribute`` key represents the relationship attribute name from model.

    ``id_attribute`` represents the attribute which stores id/s of
    relationship.

    If ``include_resource_linkage`` is **True** and ``id_attribute``
    is provided and ``include_data`` is **False**, relationship id/s are
    directly fetch from the attribute represented by ``id_attribute`` without
    actually loading the related object.
    """

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super(Relationship, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.id_attribute = kwargs.get('id_attribute', None)

    @property
    def _include_id_directly(self):
        # NOTE: property has been created because include_data can be
        # dynamically modified by the schema
        return not self.include_data \
               and self.include_resource_linkage \
               and self.id_attribute is not None

    def _get_id(self, value):
        if self._include_id_directly:
            return value

        return super(Relationship, self)._get_id(value)

    def get_value(self, obj, attr, accessor=None, default=utils.missing):
        """Return the value for a given key from an object."""
        # NOTE: Use getattr instead of direct attribute access here so that
        # subclasses aren't required to define `id_attribute` member
        if obj is None:
            return utils.missing

        if self._include_id_directly:
            # original get_value method prioritizes `attribute` key
            # thus we override it to prioritize `id_attribute` over `attribute`
            attribute = getattr(self, 'id_attribute', None)

            accessor_func = accessor or utils.get_value
            value = accessor_func(obj, attribute, default)
            return value

        if not self.include_data and not self.include_resource_linkage:
            return None

        return super(Relationship, self).get_value(
            obj, attr,
            accessor=accessor,
            default=default
        )
```

This also works well with writes. It follows the following conditions:
1. ``include_data: True`` - Follow the normal flow.
2. ``include_resource_linkage: True``, ``id_attrubute`` is not given - Follow the normal flow (will load the lazy object).
3. ``include_resource_linkage: True``, ``id_attribute`` is given - Take the value/s from the ``id_attribute`` (won't load the relationship object).
4. ``include_resource_linkage: False`` - Only give out the links (won't load the relationship object).

OK, I feel a little redeemed. The explicit `bishop88` calculation is still extremely fast.

## TL;DR:
1. `pvsystem.bishop88()` still rocks, _e.g._: 108 µs for full IV curves at 100 irradiances each with 100 points each versus 5ms for lambertw, that's a **50x speedup**!
2. `pvsystem.mpp()` still slow compared to lambertw, _e.g._: 7.28 ms for mpp at 100 irradiances versus less than 4ms for lambertw, so **2x slower** 😦 
3. Simple custom newton method gives super-duper fast results, _e.g._: 326 µs, vs 4ms, so **10x speedup** for same 100 irradiances, and the values are within default tolerance of bisection method!
4. added benefit is that newton method doesn't limit user to 1st quadrant

## Conclusion:
This may be a case where custom code is better than canned.

## test case: calculate full IV curves at 100 irradiances
Full IV curves, explicitly calculated in 108 µs for 100 points
```python
from pvlib import pvsystem
import numpy as np
IL, I0, Rs, Rsh, nNsVth = (7, 6e-7, .1, 20, .5)  # test module
voc_est = pvsystem.est_voc(IL, I0, nNsVth)  # bound the range
IL = (np.sin(np.arange(100)) + 1) * 7  # fake photocurrent dataset
vd = np.linspace(0, voc_est, 100)  # linearly spaced points
# no fancy broadcasting here
x, y = np.meshgrid(vd, IL)  # mesh the voltages and photocurrents
%timeit pvsystem.bishop88(x, y, I0, Rs, Rsh, nNsVth)
# 108 µs ± 285 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)
```

## get max power point for 100 irradiances
this is still a bit slow, remember `lambertw` does this with a lot more output in only 4ms.
```python
%timeit pvsystem.mpp(IL, I0, Rs, Rsh, nNsVth)
# 7.28 ms ± 57.6 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each)
```

## get max power point using quick and dirty custom newton method
This got me thinking, the problem here is that we are not using NumPy's ability to use L2 cache and SSE. We could try to get numba or cython or numexpr to compile optimized code before or just in time or whatever, no matter what that seems like a lot of work. We know this is a simple problem and we know exactly what to do, and we have all the tools to do it.

Just use the original newton method I originally proposed. Now we're back down to 326 µs, a 10x speedup over the existing lambertw. **This is what I meant by _way faster_!**
```python
import logging
import numpy as np

logging.basicConfig()
LOGGER = logging.getLogger(__name__)
LOGGER.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
EPS = np.finfo(np.float64).eps
DELTA = EPS**0.3
TOL = 1e-5

def mpp_fast(IL, I0, Rs, Rsh, nNsVth, tol=TOL, maxiter=50):
    args = IL, I0, Rs, Rsh, nNsVth
    voc_est = pvsystem.est_voc(IL, I0, nNsVth)
    # find the mpp
    imp_est, vmp_est, pmp_est = 0.0, 0.0, 0.0
    vd_mp = voc_est
    resnorm = np.inf  # nor of residuals
    iter = 0
    while iter < maxiter:
        imp_est, vmp_est, pmp_est, _, _, grad, grad_p, grad2p = pvsystem.bishop88(
            vd_mp, *args, gradients=True
        )
        resnorm = np.sqrt(np.sum(grad_p**2))
        if resnorm < tol:
            break
        newton_step = grad_p / grad2p
        # don't let step get crazy
        idx = np.abs(newton_step / voc_est).any() > 1.0
        newton_step[idx] = voc_est / 2.
        vd_mp -= newton_step
    return imp_est, vmp_est, pmp_est

%timeit mpp_fast(IL, I0, Rs, Rsh, nNsVth)
# 326 µs ± 1.79 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)

np.allclose(o['p_mp'], p)
# True

In [91]: np.allclose(o['i_mp'], i)
# True

In [92]: np.allclose(o['v_mp'], v)
# True
```

@chadbrewbaker: if the goal ist to obtain a single solution, we sould not need to partition the CSP . However, as described in another posting, we are interested in solving each independent CSP-component several times with different additional constraints. Small CSPs have a small number of additional constraints. Large CSPs have a large number of such constraints. Partitioning the CSP makes the solution process much more efficient. In addition, the solutions can be freely combined afterwards.

> I think that the need to reshape the output of a RecurrentLayer before being able to connect it to another layer is a bit of counter intuitiv. It would be nice if the Layer would handle this itself.

There are a few reasons for this; apart from @JackKelly's reason above, it also avoids some redundant reshapes if you are stacking recurrent layers, and it also makes the whole system more flexible with respect to how many "sample" dimensions you have vs. "feature" dimensions.  Of course, it's also very simple to write a function which does this automatically for you in your own code, if you prefer.

> Although I see the point to have a CustomRecurrentLayer I don't like it too much. How about using just the RecurrentLayer and adding options to chose the layer class to be used for input_to_hidden and hidden_to_hidden and use appropriate defaults (e.g. DenseLayer to have a fully connected recurrent layer which is the most common probably).

I'm not really seeing how this is substantially different from what we have, aside from the fact that there are currently two separate classes. Using the `CustomRecurrentLayer` you can use arbitrary layer structures for the input-to-hidden and hidden-to-hidden connections (which is more flexible than an option "to chose the layer class to be used for input_to_hidden and hidden_to_hidden") and using the `RecurrentLayer` you have "appropriate defaults" for the connections; that's exactly the point behind the way the code is now.

> Instead of having the backwards flag in all layers, I'd add a BidirectionalLayer which handles the flipping/reversing of the input and output of the backward layer and also the stacking/summing/pooling of the outputs. Although I used bidirectional networks only with RNNs, this functionality is not limited to RNNs.

As noted above, we did initially do it this way, but there was a lot of discussion to come to the format it's in now.  First, note that when `backwards=True`, the recurrent layers themselves "handles the flipping/reversing of the input and output".  Second, one nice thing about not using a `BidirectionalLayer` is that it allows the user to describe how they want the layer outputs to be combined - concatenation? Element-wise sum? Processed separately? Etc.  As I recall, there was also a strange Theano optimization bug which made things substantially slower with the `BidirecitonalLayer` I wrote, although this may have been an artifact of #104 (it was many many months ago now...)  If, for your own code, it would be convenient to have the same bidirectional structure in many places, it's straightforward to hand-roll your own as @JackKelly pointed out.

> `pad` is what's used in Caffe, so I don't think it's that nonstandard.

Ah, didn't know that. But they don't mention the topic of valid vs. full convolutions, they just implicitly do valid convolutions and allow a custom integer for `pad`.

> I'm guessing `border_mode` was adopted from scipy?

Probably even numpy: http://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/reference/generated/numpy.convolve.html
Both in numpy/scipy, it's called `mode`.

> I guess `border` and `pad` are pretty similar.

Okay, probably it's just a matter of how we formulate the docstring. Shall we go for `pad` because it's shorter?
```
pad : int, tuple of int, "full" or "same"
    By default, the kernel is only applied at positions that do not extend past the input,
    yielding an output that is smaller than the input by ``filter_shape[0] - 1`` rows and
    ``filter_shape[1] - 1`` columns (a valid convolution), when ``stride=1``.
    To change that, the input can be implicitly padded with zeros to extend the output.
    A single integer results in symmetric zero-padding of the given size on all borders,
    a tuple of two integers allows different zero-padding of rows and columns.
    ``"full"`` pads with ``filter_shape[0] - 1`` rows and ``filter_shape[1] - 1`` columns
    on both sides (a full convolution), ``"same"`` pads such that the output size matches
    the input size when ``stride=1``.
    Note that ``"full"`` and ``"same"`` can be faster than equivalent integer values.
```
Not sure if that's easy enough to understand, but it's more exact than what we had before (which we can't reuse directly if we opt for `pad`).

Comparing KLEE to angr is not a very fair comparison. KLEE operates on the source level and angr operates on the binary level.

This is fixed here! https://github.com/angr/cle/commit/6fee360b928a4f9b344c9f6d4bafae0ec1b8e712

The problem was that the relocation was performed twice, once to relocate the puts call to libc, and again to relocate it to the puts simprocedure. Since this relocation uses an addend, it depends on the original value in memory to calculate its final value. This gets changed during the first relocation, so the second relocation is wrong! The fix is to force the relocation calculations to read from the original binary data, not any modifications to it by CLE.

The real problem here is, that `UNDER_CONSTRAINED_SYMEXEC` does not necessarily make `y` symbolic. You are supposed to detect what variables are accessed on yourself, overwrite them with unconstrained symbolic values, and then perform symbolic execution.

Also, although I implemented the support for under-constrained symbolic execution in angr, please note that it is not officially supported, may not work as what the paper described, and you should not use it as a reference implementation to the original approach mentioned in the paper. Just in case you are working on a serious research project, you should either ask original authors of that paper for their code, or make sure the implementation in angr matches your expectation.

@adybbroe I've figured out the problem and have fixed part of it. I wasn't able to finish all the fixes before being done for the night. I'll get to it tomorrow. There are actually two issues here:

1. The dependency tree thinks it is being copied when you make a new Scene, but the copy doesn't seem to be correct. I don't want to rush fixing this since doing it wrong will show up later. I also have to be careful that nodes in the dependency tree are unique. For example, if a composite depends on a dataset that you also asked for (`scn.load(['comp', 'ds1'])` the tree entries for `ds1` should both be the same Node object.

2. The loading of datasets wasn't made to handle datasets added by users. So it sees a dependency tree "leaf" node (the dataset you added yourself) and expects it to have come from a reader.

So what happens is you add your new dataset to the second scene which adds a node to its dependency tree, BUT that dependency tree is actually the same dependency tree from the original Scene due to issue 1 above. Then you ask to read more datasets, it sees the node for the dataset you added to the second scene and then dies due to issue 2 above.

Regarding to your question wrt. serial number, serial number is just a RECOMMENDED where-as UDN is REQUIRED by the spec. Furthermore several vendors may be sharing serial numbers while the UDN should still remain unique. The spec contains recommendations how to generate such an UDN, although old UPnP 1.x devices may not follow these (and should still be accepted). However the latest from that series is 1.1 from 2008, so I think a growing number of devices will adhere to this newer format.

As mentioned in #707 I saw the same issue with `#pragma omp parallel for schedule(dynamic)` in a different application (here specifically: https://github.com/ros-planning/moveit_ros/issues/563). While it makes sense to try to reduce latency using OpenMP, I'd expect that the enormous additional CPU consumption we see eats up potential improvements in latency (especially if there are other processes doing CPU-heavy computation).

I didn't dig deeper into the problem (instead just remove the OpenMP parallelization), but it seems playing with different OpenMP parallelization options (static vs. dynamic, different chunkSize settings) would be a low hanging fruit to try. See https://software.intel.com/en-us/articles/performance-obstacles-for-threading-how-do-they-affect-openmp-code

As the first step I would recommend checking the value of `RS_OPTION_R200_EMITTER_ENABLED` option (should be 1) and `RS_OPTION_R200_LR_AUTO_EXPOSURE_ENABLED`. If auto-exposure is enabled, try disabling it and playing around with LR exposure and gain in `cpp-config-ui` until you reach optimal results.

@cferns I asked myself the same question a few days ago; why is there no "get_IR_frame()" call? But I finally figured it out and the following code works for me (using Python 3.6 and OpenCV 3.3), in this example to stream and display an IR image until the user hits ESC:

```
import pyrealsense2 as rs
import numpy as np
import cv2

# Configure IR stream
pipeline = rs.pipeline()
config = rs.config()
config.enable_stream(rs.stream.infrared, 640, 480, rs.format.y8, 30)

# Start input streaming
pipeline.start(config)

# Ignore first 1sec for camera warm-up
for i in range(30):
    frames = pipeline.wait_for_frames()

try:
    while True:

        # Read image
        frames = pipeline.wait_for_frames()
        infrared_frame = frames.first(rs.stream.infrared)
        IR_image = np.asanyarray(infrared_frame.get_data())

        # Display image
        cv2.imshow('IR image', IR_image)

        # Exit on ESC key
        c = cv2.waitKey(1) % 0x100
        if c == 27:
            break

finally:
    pipeline.stop() 
    cv2.destroyAllWindows()

```

`worker_cadence` needs to know the order of execution. Created issue #128 to look into an alternate way of specifying that order.

It was an issue that usually popped up if the Salt command itself was returning a non-zero response.  This caused Watchmaker to fail instead of exiting smoothly and returning a non-zero response itself.

It may be that we want it to act that way - I was thinking we might want Watchmaker to properly close up the worker cadence and then exit with a non-zero response - informing the user that Salt was producing errors.

`tb.plugins.scalar.summary(name, value, ... , y_axis=[0, None])`

y_axis would take a tuple of numbers, where None means default behavior, and a number means fix the bound to that exact number. The first number would be the lower bound, the second would be the upper bound.

Hi @loretoparisi,

Thank you for raising this issue.

For binary classification, both the softmax and sigmoid are equivalent.

For multi-label classification, using the softmax is equivalent to predicting the distribution of labels, which is a valid approach. The main difference between 2) and 3) in this case is at prediction time: for 2) the argmax is used, while for 3) either a fixed number of labels are predicted or all labels above a given threshold are predicted. Both options are supported by fastText, with the following:
```
./fasttext predict <model> <test_data> <k> <threshold>
```
In that case, fastText will predict at most `k` labels, which have a score higher than `threshold`. Thus using `threshold = 0.0` is equivalent to predicting a fixed number of labels and using `k = number of classes` is equivalent to predicting all labels with a score higher than `threshold`. In practice, `threshold` should be chosen on a validation set.

An alternative solution is to reframe the problem as one-vs-all, in which a binary classifier is trained for each label (indicating whether the label is present or not). This solution is currently not supported by fastText, and we might add it in the future. Based on experience, this approach obtains similar results to the previous one in practice.

I hope this answers your questions.

Best,
Edouard

Ok, I understand this properly now. It is, as specced, expected behaviour (though it might be a spec through omission rather than intent).

Basically, the issue comes down to the fact that `__call__`, when copying, doesn't actually update the internal arrays. The model is that a `PyFFTW` object's internal arrays are its to do what it wants with. When you call through the `__call__` interface, some checks are done as to whether the array you pass can be used as-is. If it can't, then it is copied into the internal array. The idea was that this saves overhead in creating a new array (that would need to be aligned and so on). `__call__` was never intended to be "safe" way to avoid understanding what `pyFFTW` was doing, but instead a way of not having to worry about lots of boiler plate in a fairly performance neutral manner.

In your case: for `complex64`, the odd arrays have an incorrect alignment so the copy pathway is entered. This means the copy overwrites the existing internal input array, which happened to have been set by the last call which had correct alignment (so is used as-is). The effect is the odd calls overwrite the data from the last even calls (because the even calls set the internal array). `complex128` is always aligned so the internal array is always updated (so no problem).

Now, that all said, I'm thinking perhaps the behaviour should be changed. I think I might have caught myself out with the same issue in the `interfaces` code. There is quite a low fixed overhead in creating a new array, but it is not negligible, and for small arrays might well significantly exceed the copy time (particularly if caching is working well). Maybe the answer is to have a keyword argument that defaults to a new array but allows the user to say use the internal array when necessary.

Thanks for the proposal! A few comments:

1. Tools currently take numeric indexes instead of alphabetical indexes, so we would stick to numeric indexes. I don't see much demand for implementing regular expressions for sheet names. We've only implemented regular expressions in csvgrep.
1. Only in2csv reads non-CSV data, by design. You will need to dump all the files with in2csv, then load them with csvsql. csvsql can take multiple files at once, so this wouldn't be difficult.
1. The default behavior is your `{.}`. I don't see a use case for `{}`, because `.` isn't allowed in SQL table names and makes querying more difficult due to quoting.

So, I think we'll stick to just allowing people to set sheet indices and names via `--sheets`.

Off the top of my head I can think of a few different solutions:

- Keyword to the transformer init `compute=False`, defaults to true. Programmer is responsible for this. Easy, intuitive, this would be my recommendation.
- Always return `dask.array` objects from dask based transformers, and let non-compatible scikit-learn transformers convert via `np.asarray`. This is potentially fragile if transforms are not robust to array-like objects.
- Pipeline subclass that always runs transforms collectively as a graph. Non-dask inputs to `fit/fit_transform` are coerced into dask inputs, then fed lazily through the pipeline. Non-dask transformers are run as a single task each, similar to if they were wrapped with `dask.delayed`, dask based transformers are free to use dask-array/dataframe api. Compute is called at the end of the pipeline by default to match scikit-learn eager evaluation, but a delayed option is available to support dask pipelines containing dask pipelines.

  You might implement this with either a base-class check to see if a lazy kwarg is supported, or a method check to see if a transformer supports lazy fit/transform. Something like:

  ```python
  # Using a base-class and a supported kwarg to `fit`/`fit_transform`
  if isinstance(transformer, DaskBaseEstimator):
     # lazily fit the next stage in the pipeline
     est, Xt = transformer.fit_transform(Xt, y, compute=False)
  
  # Using duck-typing and custom method names
  if hasattr(transformer, 'fit_transform_dask'):
      # lazily fit the next stage in the pipeline
      est, Xt = transformer.fit_transform_dask(Xt, y)
  ```

Of these I'd probably go with either 1 (easy, clear, intuitive) or 3 (harder to implement, may not play nice with all of scikit-learn, but probably more robust than 2).

> So, has scaler.mean_ been computed, and is it a dask.array or a numpy.array? This is a big decision.

I think that after calling `fit` in immediate mode (whether this is the default or the only option depends on the solution picked above), all attributes (e.g. `.mean_`) should be concrete. This will mesh better with scikit-learn, and matches their eager evaluation model.

I recommend that before we develop further on either solution that we play
a bit with concrete problems to see what is important.

Eventually my guess is that this implementation will grow to the point
where you can use it as a piece to build out the full algorithm that you
want without having to do any low-level dask work.  However, I'm going to
claim that we don't yet know what algorithm you'll want to build before
first having a few more serious examples to play with.

I definitely agree with both the `detail` and `cast` reorganization. I think there's one more thing that might be worth considering as this reorganization is taking place: going from header-only to optionally header-only. For example, see [opt-in header-only libraries](https://vittorioromeo.info/index/blog/2016_cpp_library_configuration_api.html) (but in the case of pybind11, it would be _opt-out_). I bring this up here because this would influence some of the header organization. 

There are more details in the linked blog post, but I'll summarize what it would look like for pybind11 (it's actually slightly simplified since pybind11 would only support header-only and static lib configurations but **not** dynamic lib).

### Optional header-only

This would make it possible to compile pybind11 itself as a static library which would help with compile time. This can be done so that it's 100% backward compatible -- existing users would not notice any change and everything would still function as header-only by default. Compiling as a static library would be opt-in, e.g. by defining `PYBIND11_STATIC_LIB`.

The main idea is that headers would be split up into the expected `.h` and `.cpp` files and follow completely normal C++ development. The only departure would be that all functions would need to annotated with a macro, e.g. `PYBIND11_FUNC` that looks like this:
```c++
#if !defined(PYBIND11_STATIC_LIB)
# define PYBIND11_FUNC inline
#else
# define PYBIND11_FUNC
#endif
```
This would essentially replace `inline` which is currently required for all non-template functions.

The header and source files would look like this:
```c++
// foo.h
#pragma once

PYBIND11_FUNC void foo();

#if !defined(PYBIND11_STATIC_LIB)
# include "foo.cpp"
#endif
```

```c++
// foo.cpp
#include "foo.h"

PYBIND11_FUNC void foo() {
    // implementation
}
```

The obvious benefit would be compile time. I made a very quick prototype to measure the speedup that could be expected. I only moved `generic_type` and `cpp_function` into the static lib and got the numbers below. Note there is a lot more non-template code that could be moved into the static lib. This is measured by compiling `pybind11_tests`:

```
macOS/clang 8.0.0
-----------------
header-only
  time make: 96.01s user 3.03s system 121% cpu 1:21.71 total
  file size: 1460552

static lib (prototype -- most of the library is still header-only)
  time make: 77.64s user 2.79s system 120% cpu 1:06.86 total
  file size: 1468768

--> time: +23% faster compilation
--> size: +0.56% (+8216 bytes) larger binary


macOS/GCC 6.3.0
---------------
header-only
  time make: 130.98s user 6.10s system 99% cpu 2:17.59 total
  file size: 1931124

static lib (prototype -- most of the library is still header-only)
  time make: 102.53s user 5.57s system 99% cpu 1:48.59 total
  file size: 1884812

--> time: +28% faster compilation
--> size: -2.40% (-46312 bytes) smaller binary
```

The performance would still go up as more of the files split up into `.h`/`.cpp` pairs. The speedup also becomes larger as the number of translation units is increased, as would be expected. The binary size goes up with clang, but down with GCC. I'd expect that should go down slightly and the regression with clang is most likely due to the quick prototype that this was measured with.

Most of the `.h`/`.cpp` split-up is fairly straightforward. It would just need to be coordinated with the reorganization above to avoid moving the files twice.

Thoughts?

The removal was intentional as part of the breaking change in PR #425, but I forgot to explicitly mention it in the PR, which is a pretty big oversight on my part. However, I do stand by the removal. Breaking the compilation or at least adding a deprecation warning is very important here -- otherwise it's a silent breaking change at runtime which is not good at all (`throw` instead of `nullptr`).

The accessors have so far not been documented beyond one or two small mentions. I would imagine that anyone using the current `operator bool` actually found out about it by looking at internal code (it's not even used in the tests). Therefore, breaking this is likely to have minimal impact, but a compile time break is preferable to a silent runtime break.

There are several more reasons for removing `operator bool` which I'll get into below, but the main point is that there must be at least something at compile time. If it must be restored, I would suggest:

  1. `operator bool` should at least be marked as deprecated -- to generate a warning.
  2. The `list`/`tuple` accessors should not gain `operator bool` since they did not have one before. Change: `bool tuple_item::has() = delete;`


### Issues with `accessor::operator bool()`

Originally, only half of the accessors actually had `operator bool`: `attr`/`item` vs. `list`/`tuple`. This is inconsistent, especially since the last 3 accessors share the same `operator []` syntax, but behave differently. Of course, one possibility is to give them all `operator bool`, but that brings other issues.

Having `accessor::operator bool()` behave like `hasattr`/`contains` is very surprising compared to Python:
```python
l = list()
...
if 42 in l:  # contains item
    ...
if l[42]:  # check if item 42 is true
    ...
```

With `operator bool`, the C++ API uses the same syntax as the latter, but actually does the former:
```c++
if (l[42]) {} // actually does: contains item
              // expected: check if item 42 is true
```

Having this kind of inconsistency between Python and C++ is pretty bad especially given the similarity of the syntax.

There is an additional inconsistency to `operator bool` which is entirely internal to C++:
```c++
// get object first
object foo = obj.attr("foo"); // throw on error
if (foo) { // always true
	...
}

// direct check
if (obj.attr("foo")) { // may be true or false
    ...	
}
```

It's completely reasonable to expect that those two conditions do the same thing, but with `accessor::operator bool()` that would not be the case, as indicated by the comments.

To add more info here, we want a flag (or flags) in the core of the client (not in any of the plugins) that can be used for challenge selection. Flags like `--standalone-supported-challenges` and `--manual-supported-challenges` should be removed.

Probably the best way for this to work is to have a flag like `--preferred-challenges` or `--challenge-preferences` that can be used to provide a delimited list of the preferred challenges you want Certbot to perform, listed in the order they are preferred. This is like the `--standalone-support-challenges` flag.

Normally, Certbot determines that challenges that will be performed with the `gen_challenge_path` function in `certbot/auth_handler.py`. `get_chall_pref` is called on the selected plugin and is passed as the `preferences` argument to this function, which then uses these preferences and the challenges required by the server to determine which challenge to perform.

With the addition of the flag described above, the `preferences` parameter to this function should be the intersection of the challenges provided by the user in the new flag and the return value of `get_chall_pref`, with the order of the challenges being determined the user in the new flag.

For example, if the user provides `--preferred-challenges dns-01,tls-sni-01,http-01` and `get_chall_pref` returns `[tls-sni-01,dns-01]`, the value for `preferences` should be `[dns-01,tls-sni-01]`. Note: `gen_challenge_path` expects `acme.challenges.Challenge` classes, not simple strings as shown in my example.

If the intersection between the challenges requested by the user and those preferred by the plugin is empty, Certbot should error out (this may happen automatically, but this is the desired end result as there is no way to satisfy the CA and the user).

This issue also requests the ability to specify ports which is available through the `--http-01-port` and `--tls-sni-01-port` flags. Not much (or anything?) has to be done here, except maybe move them out of the testing section of help and fix up their documentation.

> Can you go a bit more in detail on why the nonce management is useless and create an follow-up issue for it.

Either we assume that we are share the privatekey or not:

- if we don't share it then the nonce update is useless: Since our node is the only one sending transactions, therefor the only node changing the nonce, so the local nonce is always valid.
   - assuming that transactions go to pending *faster* than the node restarts.
- if we do share, then there **will be** races where two nodes send the different transactions with the same nonce, only updating the nonce without fixing and resending the transactions is not sufficient. At the best case scenario, the retry logic would also fix the transaction, but then either:
  - the two nodes deadlock each other and waste ether, because one will try to overwrite the other transaction and needs to pay more for the gas
  - the node to send the second transaction accepts that it lost the race and send a new transaction with a fresher nonce. This is IMO just too annoying the effort and would be better to just /not/ use local signing.

SVHN Dataset: http://ufldl.stanford.edu/housenumbers/
The classification dataset has pre-cropped numbers, ranging from 0 to 9.
The task is to write a data loader similar to [CIFAR-10](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/master/torchvision/datasets/cifar.py#L15-L155) that can load the SVHN dataset.

I see two options on how to do that at the zhmc CLI:

Option 1:
* An option `--XXX` for a property that can be set to null gets a corresponding `-no-XXX` option. That would probably be the minority of options.
* An `--XXX` option and its corresponding `-no-XXX` option are mutually exclusive.

Option 2:
* An option `--XXX` for a property that can be set to null accepts a special string value 'null'. If there are string typed properties that can be set to null, and the string "null" is valid as a normal value, then we can add an option `--null-value YYYY` that sets the value one has to specify for an option to set the property to null, to `YYYY`.

@AdamGleave originally I was thinking of something like the following:

1. Keep a "pending nodes" counter in the autoscaler which tracks outstanding instance creation requests.
2. When checking whether to create new nodes, compare num_running + num_pending to the target.
3. When checking whether to remove nodes, compare against just num_running.

The idea to just avoid launching more than 1 instance creation request at a time could also work though, and might be simpler, since it only involves only adding a single boolean to the autoscaler state. I would do this in the autoscaler logic itself instead of the node provider so that it will work with other providers (perhaps create a new node provider object instance for the instance launcher thread, to avoid potential thread safety issues).

ffmpeg's tee pseudo-muxer seems to be broken... HOWEVER this seems to do what I want:

streamlink -O ... | \
        ffmpeg \
        -i - \
        -bsf:a aac_adtstoasc \
        -map 0:0 -map 0:1 \
        -c:a copy -c:v copy \
        -c copy rip-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S).mkv \ 
        -y -f mpegts -c copy - | \
        mpv --cache=64 -

The reason for using numeric IDs in the map is that matroska files don't seem to support saving a timed ID3 stream XD.

mpegts might not be the best output format, but none of the others that are listed in the ffmpeg documentation (that looked remotely fitting anyway) actually worked.

-y is an artifact from when I was using /dev/stdout as the output... but it should be harmless.

-c can be invoked a seemingly unlimited number of times, each output is it's own muxing stage; from ffmpegs output, -c is actually some syntax sugar for a more complex filtergraph that's duplicating the inputs and assigning outputs to many files.

Here's a mild annoyance that happens if we make `port` and friends become mandatory kwargs instead of positional args – it becomes impossible to start a background server without using `partial`:

```python
# Before
await nursery.start(serve_tcp, handler, port), but I hadn't noticed it before 

# After
await nursery.start(partial(serve_tcp, handler, port=port))
```

This isn't a huge showstopper or anything, but I missed it before, and it is a bit annoying given how commonly these two operations are combined. And that the original reason @dstufft suggested making this change was that he wanted to avoid using `partial` :-). In fact for those actually using the new `*args` support, I guess the relevant comparison might be:

```python
# Before
await nursery.start(serve_tcp, partial(handler, *handler_args), port)

# After
await nursery.start(partial(serve_tcp, handler, *handler_args, port=port)
```

Currently studio focuses on providing a platform neutral tool for ENN and ML.  The design targets a master and distributed worker approach with storage and queuing as the two dependencies that have any coupling into our experiments.  

Within Sentient we do use k8s along with our own compute infrastructure, and layer studioml on top of this.  In the past our focus was to integrate the cloud offerings into the studioml code base, these implementations still exist and are used today.  Having said that, we have begun using Minio for S3 and RabbitMQ for queuing to create a reference deployment in preference to having cloud provider dependencies.  This has worked very well for us.  In these case k8s is used as the platform and we leverage kops, kubeadm et al to provide the infrastructure.  The main emphasis here is that generic k8s is being used.

The above is a Sentient perspective which could easily be changed, and is a narrow one given several others are using studioml and might be seeking different directions for their deployments and use of studioml.

> Since we don't compare the contents of flash with the connectivity firmware

Is there any downside to doing so?

I mean, the current implementation looks like:

```
def thread(...):
    # ...
    if flasher.fw_check():
        click.echo("OK.")
    if flash_connectivity:
        flasher = NCPFlasher(serial_port=port, snr = jlink_snr)
        else:
            flasher.fw_flash()
            click.echo("Connectivity firmware flashed.")
    
    dfu.trigger(...)
```

What if, instead of that, we do something more like...

```
def thread(...):
    # ...
    
    flasher = NCPFlasher(serial_port=port, snr = jlink_snr)
    if flasher.fw_check():
        click.echo("Board already flashed with connectivity firmware.")
    else:
        if flash_connectivity:
            flasher.fw_flash()
            click.echo("Connectivity firmware flashed.")
        else:
            click.echo("Board is not flashed with connectivity firmware. Try again, using the -f option.")
            sys.exit(-1)
    dfu.trigger(...)
```

This way we could check independently whether we got a connectivity IC, and whether the connectivity IC is responding to reset commands.

I'll think about namespaces first...

I'm struggling to reach an impassioned (or even reasoned) conclusion as to whether, as an external user, I'd prefer everything to be

i) reachable from a single `galsim::` namespace 
ii) split up into multiple single layer namespaces (e.g.) `galsim::`, `sbp::`, `hsm::`
iii) tiered into `galsim::`, `galsim::sbp`, `galsim::hsm` etc.

But I have had a couple of thoughts:

i) seems to me like we lose the nice property of namespaces for being able to organize program units into their broad functions, which I think probably helps developers more than the convenience of being able to use `galsim::` for everything... `hsm::` is sufficiently different enough from `sbp::` that I think it makes a great deal of sense to separate them.

That leaves ii) and iii):  I can't really put much between them.  I think ii) is squeaking it for me simply because of the convenience of not having `galsim::` there every time, but conversely I could also possibly see the unifying benefits that having the `galsim::` overarching namespace would give.  If I was writing some code and using a few libraries, not just galsim, then having all the galsim stuff grouped together like this is conceivably of some value in terms of clarity... Small, but arguably not zero value.

I'm afraid you two chaps are going to have to duke this one out according to who cares most :)  So I've assigned you both to this issue to decide...


Regarding directory (& thus include locations)

It didn't seem to me there was so much of a division on this question, are we happy to go for Jim's schema? I think I agree this might be best minus the meas directory, at least for the time being -- we might want to put it back later if something else that's a "meas" package comes along. 

As for the includes being an optional `include GalSim.h` for ease for external developers, and for the rest of us a more strict and explicit (e.g.) `include galsim/SBProfile.h` showing precisely what modules are being used... I think that is a pretty good compromise between functionality (for them) and clarity (from us).  Jim, do you agree?
